audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman talking to a doctor at a clinic about a medical problem.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, come and take a seat.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: My name is Carl Rogers, and I'm one of the doctors here at the Total Health Clinic.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So, I understand this is your first visit to the clinic.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, well I hope you'll be very happy with the service you receive here.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So if it's all right with you, I'll take a few details to help me give you the best possible service.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So, can I check first of all that we have the correct personal details for you?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So your full name is Julie Anne Garcia.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: That's correct.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: The woman's name is Julie Anne Garcia.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Garcia has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, come and take a seat.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: My name is Carl Rogers, and I'm one of the doctors here at the Total Health Clinic.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So, I understand this is your first visit to the clinic.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, well I hope you'll be very happy with the service you receive here.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So if it's all right with you, I'll take a few details to help me give you the best possible service.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So can I check first of all that we have the correct personal details for you?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So your full name is Julie Anne Garcia.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: That's correct.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: And can I have a contact phone number?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: OK, and then can I just check that we have the correct date of birth?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: October 10th, 1992.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, I actually have 1991. I'll just correct that now.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Right, so that's all good.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Now I just need a few more personal details.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Do you have an occupation, either full-time or part-time?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, I work full time in Esterhazy's, you know the restaurant chain.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I started off as a waitress there a few years ago, and I'm a manager now.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, I know them.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Yeah, they're down on 114th Street, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Uh. That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I've been there a few times.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: I just love their salads.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: That's good to hear.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Right, so one more thing I need to know before we talk about why you're here, Julie, and that's the name of your insurance company.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: It's Cawley Life Insurance, that's CAWLEY.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Excellent, thank you so much.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, Julie, let's look at how we can help you.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So, tell me a little about what brought you here today.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Well, I've been getting a pain in my knee, the left one, not very serious at first, but it's gotten worse.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So I thought I ought to see someone about it.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: That's certainly the right decision.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: So how long have you been aware of this pain?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Is it just a few days, or is it longer than that?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Longer.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: It's been worse for the last couple of days, but it's 3 weeks since I first noticed it.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: It came on quite gradually though, so I kind of ignored it at first.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: And have you taken any medication yourself or treated it in any way?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Um. Yeah, I've been taking medication to deal with the pain, tylenol, and that works OK for a few hours.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: But I don't like to keep taking it.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, and what about heat treatment?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Have you tried applying heat at all?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: No, but I have been using ice on it for the last few days.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: And does that seem to help the pain at all?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: A little, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Now you look as if you're quite fit normally.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: I am, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: So, do you do any sport on a regular basis?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Yes, I play a lot of tennis.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I belong to a club, so I go there a lot.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I'm quite competitive, so I enjoy that side of it as well as the exercise, but I haven't gone since this started.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: And do you do any other types of exercise?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Uh. Yeah, I sometimes do a little swimming.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: But usually just when I'm on vacation, but normally I go running a few times a week, maybe 3 or 4 times.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. So your legs are getting quite a pounding, but you haven't had any problems up to now.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: No, not with my legs.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I did have an accident last year when I slipped and hurt my shoulder, but that's better now.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: And do you have any allergies?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: No, none that I'm aware of.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: And do you take any medication on a regular basis?
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: Well, I take vitamins, but that's all.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I'm generally very healthy.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, well, let's have a closer look and see what might be causing this problem.
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: If you can just get up...
audioscript.speaker: CARL

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a guide talking to a group of tourists who are about to visit an English castle.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We'll be arriving at Branley Castle in about five minutes, but before we get there, I'll give you a little information about the castle, and what our visit will include.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, in fact, there's been a castle on this site for over eleven hundred years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first building was a fort, constructed in 914 AD, for defense against Danish invaders.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By King Alfred the Great's daughter, who ruled England at the time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the following century, after the Normans conquered England, the land was given to a nobleman called Richard de Vere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And he built a castle there that stayed in the de Vere family for over 400 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, when Queen Elizabeth I (Queen Elizabeth the first) announced that she was going to visit the castle in 1576, it was beginning to look a bit run down, and it was decided that rather than repair the guest rooms,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: they'd make a new house for her, out of wood, next to the main hall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She stayed there for four nights, and apparently it was very luxurious.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But unfortunately, it was destroyed a few years later by fire.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 17th century, the castle belonged to the wealthy Fenys family, who enlarged it and made it more comfortable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, by 1982, the Fenys family could no longer afford to maintain the castle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Even though they received government support, and they put it on the market.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was eventually taken over by a company who owned a number of amusement parks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But when we get there, I think you'll see that they've managed to retain the original atmosphere of the castle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When you go inside, you'll find that in the State Rooms, there are lifelike moving wax models dressed in costumes of different periods in the past.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which even carry on conversations together.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as that, in every room, there are booklets giving information about what the room was used for, and the history of the objects and furniture it contains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The castle park's quite extensive. At one time sheep were kept there, and in the 19th century the owners had a little zoo with animals like rabbits and even a baby elephant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nowadays the old zoo buildings are used for public displays of paintings and sculpture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The park also has some beautiful trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though the oldest of all, which dated back 800 years, was sadly blown down in 1987.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now you're free to wander around on your own until 4:30.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But then at the end of our visit, we'll all meet together at the bottom of the great staircase.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll then go on to the Long Gallery, where there's a wonderful collection of photographs showing the family who owned the castle 100 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Having tea and cakes in the conservatory, and we'll then take you to the same place where afternoon tea will be served to you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, if you can take a look at your plans, you'll see Branley Castle has 4 towers, joined together by a high wall, with the river on two sides.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Don't miss seeing the Great Hall, that's near the river in the main tower, the biggest one, which was extended and redesigned in the 18th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you want to get a good view of the whole castle, you can walk around the walls.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The starting point is quite near the main entrance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Walk straight down the path until you get to the south gate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's just there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Don't go on to the north gate, there's no way up from there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There'll shortly be a show in which you can see archers displaying their skill with a bow and arrow.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The quickest way to get there is to take the first left.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After the main entrance, and follow the path past the bridge, then you'll see it in front of you at the end.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you like animals, there's also a display of hunting birds, falcons and eagles and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you go from the main entrance in the direction of the south gate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But turn right before you get there, instead of going through it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll see it on your right, past the first tower.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At 3 pm, there's a short performance of traditional dancing on the outdoor stage.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's right at the other side of the castle from the entrance and over the bridge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's about 10 minutes' walk or so. And finally the shop.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's actually inside one of the towers, but the way in is from the outside.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just take the first left after the main entrance, go down the path, and take the first right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's got some lovely gifts and souvenirs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, so we're just arriving.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, in fact, there's been a castle on this site for over eleven hundred years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The first building was a fort, constructed in 914 AD, for defense against Danish invaders.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By King Alfred the Great's daughter, who ruled England at the time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the following century, after the Normans conquered England, the land was given to a nobleman called Richard de Vere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And he built a castle there that stayed in the de Vere family for over 400 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, when Queen Elizabeth I (Queen Elizabeth the first) announced that she was going to visit the castle in 1576, it was beginning to look a bit run down, and it was decided that rather than repair the guest rooms,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: they'd make a new house for her, out of wood, next to the main hall.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She stayed there for four nights, and apparently it was very luxurious.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But unfortunately, it was destroyed a few years later by fire.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the 17th century, the castle belonged to the wealthy Fenys family, who enlarged it and made it more comfortable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, by 1982, the Fenys family could no longer afford to maintain the castle.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Even though they received government support, and they put it on the market.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was eventually taken over by a company who owned a number of amusement parks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But when we get there, I think you'll see that they've managed to retain the original atmosphere of the castle.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When you go inside, you'll find that in the State Rooms, there are lifelike moving wax models dressed in costumes of different periods in the past.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which even carry on conversations together.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as that, in every room, there are booklets giving information about what the room was used for, and the history of the objects and furniture it contains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The castle park's quite extensive. At one time sheep were kept there, and in the 19th century the owners had a little zoo with animals like rabbits and even a baby elephant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nowadays the old zoo buildings are used for public displays of paintings and sculpture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The park also has some beautiful trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Though the oldest of all, which dated back 800 years, was sadly blown down in 1987.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now you're free to wander around on your own until 4:30.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But then at the end of our visit, we'll all meet together at the bottom of the great staircase.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We'll then go on to the Long Gallery, where there's a wonderful collection of photographs showing the family who owned the castle 100 years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Having tea and cakes in the conservatory, and we'll then take you to the same place where afternoon tea will be served to you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, if you can take a look at your plans, you'll see Branley Castle has 4 towers, joined together by a high wall, with the river on two sides.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Don't miss seeing the Great Hall, that's near the river in the main tower, the biggest one, which was extended and redesigned in the 18th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you want to get a good view of the whole castle, you can walk around the walls.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The starting point is quite near the main entrance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Walk straight down the path until you get to the south gate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it's just there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Don't go on to the north gate, there's no way up from there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There'll shortly be a show in which you can see archers displaying their skill with a bow and arrow.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The quickest way to get there is to take the first left.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After the main entrance, and follow the path past the bridge, then you'll see it in front of you at the end.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you like animals, there's also a display of hunting birds, falcons and eagles and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you go from the main entrance in the direction of the south gate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But turn right before you get there, instead of going through it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll see it on your right, past the first tower.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At 3 pm, there's a short performance of traditional dancing on the outdoor stage.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's right at the other side of the castle from the entrance and over the bridge.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's about 10 minutes' walk or so. And finally the shop.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's actually inside one of the towers, but the way in is from the outside.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Just take the first left after the main entrance, go down the path, and take the first right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's got some lovely gifts and souvenirs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right, so we're just arriving.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Before Queen Elizabeth I visited the castle in 1576,
options: repairs were carried out to the guest rooms.
a new building was constructed for her.
a fire damaged part of the main hall.

items.title: In 1982, the castle was sold to
options: the government.
the Fenys family.
an entertainment company.

items.title: In some of the rooms, visitors can
options: speak to experts on the history of the castle.
interact with actors dressed as famous characters.
see models of historical figures moving and talking.

items.title: In the castle park, visitors can
options: see an 800-year-old tree.
go to an art exhibition.
visit a small zoo.

items.title: At the end of the visit, the group will have
options: afternoon tea in the conservatory.
the chance to meet the castle's owners.
a photograph together on the Great Staircase.

items: Starting point for walking the walls <input>

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two environmental science students called Rosie and Martin discussing their presentation on an extinct animal called the woolly Mammoth with their tutor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Rosie and Martin, let's look at what you've got for your presentation on woolly mammoths.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, we've got a short outline here.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Thanks, uh, so, it's about a research project in North America.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, but we thought we needed something general about woolly mammoths in our introduction.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: To establish that they were related to our modern elephant, and they lived thousands of years ago in the last ice age.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Maybe we could show a video clip of a cartoon about mammoths.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: But that would be a bit childish. Or we could have a diagram, it could be a timeline to show when they lived, with illustrations?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Or we could just show a drawing of them walking in the ice?
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: No, let's go with your last suggestion.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then you're describing the discovery of the mammoth tooth on St Paul's Island in Alaska.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And why it was significant?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, the tooth was found by a man called Russell Graham.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: He picked it up from under a rock in a cave.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: He knew it was special.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: For a start, it was in really good condition, as if it had been just extracted from the animal's jawbone.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Anyway, they found it was 6,500 years old.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: So why was that significant?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, the Mammoth bones previously found on the North American mainland were much less recent than that.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: So this was really amazing.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Then we're making an animated diagram to show the geography of the area in prehistoric times.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: So originally, St Paul's Island wasn't an island.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It was connected to the mainland, and mammoths and other animals like bears were able to roam around the whole area.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Then the climate warmed up, and the sea level began to rise, and the island got cut off from the mainland.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: So those mammoths on the island couldn't escape.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: They had to stay on the island.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: And in fact, the species survived there for thousands of years after they'd become extinct on the mainland.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: So, why do you think they died out on the mainland?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: No one's sure.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Anyway, next we'll explain how Graham and his team identified the date when the mammoths became extinct on the island.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: They concluded that the extinction happened 5,600 years ago, which is a very precise time for a prehistoric extinction.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It's based on samples they took from MUD at the bottom of a lake on the island.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: They analyzed it to find out what had fallen in over time - bits of plants, volcanic ash,
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: and even DNA from the mammoths themselves.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It's standard procedure, but it took nearly two years to do.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So why don't you quickly go through the main sections of your presentation and discuss what action's needed for each part?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, so for the introduction, we're using a visual.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: So once we've prepared that, we're done.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I think we need to write down all the ideas we want to include here, not just rely on memory.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: How we begin the presentation is so important.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, you're right.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The discovery of the Mammoth tooth is probably the most dramatic part, but we don't have that much information.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Only what we got from the online article.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I thought maybe we could get in touch with the researcher who led the team, and ask him to tell us a bit more.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Great idea.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: What about the section with the initial questions asked by the researchers?
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: We've got a lot on that, but we need to make it interesting.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: We could ask the audience to suggest some questions about it, and then see how many of them we can answer.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I don't think it would take too long.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that would add a bit of variety.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then the section on further research carried out on the island, analyzing the MUD in the lake.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: I wonder if we've actually got too much information here.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Should we cut some?
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: I don't think so, but it's all a bit muddled at present.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, maybe it would be better if it followed a chronological pattern.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: I think so.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: The findings and possible explanations section is just about ready, but we need to practice it, so we're sure it won't overrun.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I think it should be OK.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: But yes, ha, let's make sure.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Hmm. In the last section, relevance to the present day, you've got some good ideas, but this is where you need to move away from the ideas of others and give your own viewpoint.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, we'll think about that.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Now shall we...
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: How will Rosie and Martin introduce their presentation?
options: with a drawing of woolly mammoths in their natural habitat
with a timeline showing when woolly mammoths lived
with a video clip about woolly mammoths

items.title: What was surprising about the mammoth tooth found by Russell Graham?
options: It was still embedded in the mammoth's jawbone.
It was from an unknown species of mammoth.
It was not as old as mammoth remains from elsewhere.

items.title: The students will use an animated diagram to demonstrate how the mammoths
options: became isolated on the island.
spread from the island to other areas.
coexisted with other animals on the island.

items.title: According to Martin, what is unusual about the date of the mammoths' extinction on the island?
options: how exact it is
how early it is
how it was established

body.title: Woolly mammoths on St Paul's Island

text: What action will the students take for each of the following sections of their presentation?
textReadable: What action will the students take for each of the following sections of their presentation?

items: Initial questions asked by the researchers <input>
Further research carried out on the island <input>
body.title: Sections of presentation

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a lecture about the history of weather forecasting.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In this series of lectures about the history of weather forecasting, I'll start by examining its early history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That'll be the subject of today's talk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, so we'll start by going back thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most ancient cultures had weather gods, and weather catastrophes such as floods, played an important role in many creation myths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Generally, weather was attributed to the whims of the gods, as the wide range of weather gods in various cultures shows.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance, there's the Egyptian sun god Ra, and Thor, the Norse god of thunder and lightning.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many ancient civilizations developed rites such as dances,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: in order to make the weather gods look kindly on them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the weather was of daily importance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Observing the skies and drawing the correct conclusions from these observations was really important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, their survival depended on it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It isn't known when people first started to observe the skies, but at around 650 BC, the Babylonians produced the first short range weather forecasts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Based on their observations of clouds and other phenomena.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Chinese also recognized weather patterns, and by 300 BC, astronomers had developed a calendar, which divided the year into 24 festivals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each associated with a different weather phenomenon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ancient Greeks were the first to develop a more scientific approach to explaining the weather.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The work of the philosopher and scientist Aristotle in the fourth century BC is especially noteworthy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As his ideas held sway for nearly 2,000 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 340 BC, he wrote a book in which he attempted to account for the formation of rain, clouds, wind, and storms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He also described celestial phenomena such as haloes - that is, bright circles of light around the sun, the moon, and bright stars - and comets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many of his observations were surprisingly accurate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, he believed that heat could cause water to evaporate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But he also jumped to quite a few wrong conclusions, such as that winds are breathed out by the earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Errors like this were rectified from the Renaissance onwards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For nearly 2,000 years, Aristotle's work was accepted as the chief authority on weather theory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Alongside this, though, in the Middle Ages, weather observations were passed on in the form of proverbs, such as "Red sky at night, shepherd's delight; red sky in the morning, shepherd's warning".
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many of these are based on very good observations, and are accurate, as contemporary meteorologists have discovered.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For centuries, any attempt to forecast the weather could only be based on personal observations, but in the 15th century, scientists began to see the need for instruments. Until then, the only ones available were weather vanes to determine the wind direction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And early versions of rain gauges.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the first invented in the 15th century was a hygrometer, which measured humidity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was one of many inventions that contributed to the development of weather forecasting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1592, the Italian scientist and inventor Galileo developed the world's first thermometer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: His student Torricelli later invented the barometer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which allowed people to measure atmospheric pressure.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1648, the French philosopher Pascal proved that pressure decreases with altitude.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This discovery was verified by English astronomer Halley in 1686.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Halley was also the first person to map trade winds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This increasing ability to measure factors related to weather helped scientists to understand the atmosphere and its processes better, and they started collecting weather observation data systematically.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 18th century, the scientist and politician Benjamin Franklin carried out work on electricity and lightning in particular, but he was also very interested in weather.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And studied it throughout most of his life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was Franklin who discovered that storms generally travel from west to east.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition to new meteorological instruments, other developments contributed to our understanding of the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People in different locations began to keep records.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in the mid 19th century, the invention of the telegraph made it possible for these records to be collated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This led by the end of the 19th century to the first weather services.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was not until the early 20th century that mathematics and physics became part of meteorology, and we'll continue from that point next week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a lecture about the history of weather forecasting.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In this series of lectures about the history of weather forecasting, I'll start by examining its early history.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That'll be the subject of today's talk.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, so we'll start by going back thousands of years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most ancient cultures had weather gods, and weather catastrophes such as floods, played an important role in many creation myths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Generally, weather was attributed to the whims of the gods, as the wide range of weather gods in various cultures shows.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance, there's the Egyptian sun god Ra, and Thor, the Norse god of thunder and lightning.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many ancient civilizations developed rites such as dances,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: in order to make the weather gods look kindly on them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the weather was of daily importance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Observing the skies and drawing the correct conclusions from these observations was really important.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, their survival depended on it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It isn't known when people first started to observe the skies, but at around 650 BC, the Babylonians produced the first short range weather forecasts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Based on their observations of clouds and other phenomena.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Chinese also recognized weather patterns, and by 300 BC, astronomers had developed a calendar, which divided the year into 24 festivals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each associated with a different weather phenomenon.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ancient Greeks were the first to develop a more scientific approach to explaining the weather.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The work of the philosopher and scientist Aristotle in the fourth century BC is especially noteworthy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As his ideas held sway for nearly 2,000 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 340 BC, he wrote a book in which he attempted to account for the formation of rain, clouds, wind, and storms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He also described celestial phenomena such as haloes - that is, bright circles of light around the sun, the moon, and bright stars - and comets.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many of his observations were surprisingly accurate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, he believed that heat could cause water to evaporate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But he also jumped to quite a few wrong conclusions, such as that winds are breathed out by the earth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Errors like this were rectified from the Renaissance onwards.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For nearly 2,000 years, Aristotle's work was accepted as the chief authority on weather theory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Alongside this, though, in the Middle Ages, weather observations were passed on in the form of proverbs, such as "Red sky at night, shepherd's delight; red sky in the morning, shepherd's warning".
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many of these are based on very good observations, and are accurate, as contemporary meteorologists have discovered.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For centuries, any attempt to forecast the weather could only be based on personal observations, but in the 15th century, scientists began to see the need for instruments. Until then, the only ones available were weather vanes to determine the wind direction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And early versions of rain gauges.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the first invented in the 15th century was a hygrometer, which measured humidity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was one of many inventions that contributed to the development of weather forecasting.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1592, the Italian scientist and inventor Galileo developed the world's first thermometer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: His student Torricelli later invented the barometer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which allowed people to measure atmospheric pressure.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1648, the French philosopher Pascal proved that pressure decreases with altitude.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This discovery was verified by English astronomer Halley in 1686.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And Halley was also the first person to map trade winds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This increasing ability to measure factors related to weather helped scientists to understand the atmosphere and its processes better, and they started collecting weather observation data systematically.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the 18th century, the scientist and politician Benjamin Franklin carried out work on electricity and lightning in particular, but he was also very interested in weather.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And studied it throughout most of his life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was Franklin who discovered that storms generally travel from west to east.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition to new meteorological instruments, other developments contributed to our understanding of the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People in different locations began to keep records.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And in the mid 19th century, the invention of the telegraph made it possible for these records to be collated.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This led by the end of the 19th century to the first weather services.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was not until the early 20th century that mathematics and physics became part of meteorology, and we'll continue from that point next week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
